content.introduction: This section introduces the foundational ideas behind data structures and algorithms, focusing on how data is organized, accessed, and analyzed for performance.

sections.title: Data Structures
sections.content: A data structure is a way to organize and store data so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. Choices include linear structures (arrays, lists) and non-linear structures (trees, graphs).
examples: Arrays for contiguous storage
Linked lists for flexible insertions
Trees for hierarchical data

sections.title: Abstract Data Types (ADT)
sections.content: An ADT defines a data model by its operations and behavior, without specifying its implementation. The same ADT can have multiple implementations with different trade-offs.
examples: Stack ADT implemented with arrays or lists
Queue ADT implemented with circular buffers

sections.title: Algorithm Analysis
sections.content: Algorithm analysis estimates resource usage, typically time and space, to compare approaches and predict scalability.
examples: Binary search uses logarithmic time
Linear search scales linearly

content.introduction: This section introduces the fundamental concepts that form the foundation of data structures and algorithms. Understanding these concepts is crucial for mastering computer science and developing efficient software solutions.

sections.title: 1.1.1 Data Structures
sections.content:
Definition: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data in a computer so that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently.

Key Characteristics:
1) Organization refers to how data elements are arranged and related to each other.
2) Access Methods determine how data can be retrieved and modified.
3) Memory Usage indicates how efficiently memory is utilized.
4) Operations define the allowed operations that can be performed on the data.

Types of Data Structures:
1) Primitive Data Structures include basic data types like integers, floats, characters, and booleans.
2) Linear Data Structures have elements arranged in sequential order such as arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues.
3) Non-linear Data Structures have elements not arranged sequentially like trees, graphs, and heaps.
4) Homogeneous structures contain all elements of the same type while Heterogeneous structures contain elements of different types.

Classification by Implementation:
Static Data Structures have fixed size determined at compile time like arrays. Dynamic Data Structures can change size during runtime like linked lists and trees.
examples: Array with five elements showing linear, homogeneous, static properties
Linked List with nodes connected sequentially showing linear, dynamic properties
Binary Tree showing hierarchical structure with parent-child relationships
Hash Table showing key-value pairs with hash function mapping

sections.title: 1.1.2 Abstract Data Types (ADT)
sections.content:
Definition: An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a theoretical concept that defines a data type by its behavior (operations) rather than its implementation.

Key Properties:
1) Data Abstraction hides internal implementation details.
2) Encapsulation bundles data and operations together.
3) Interface defines what operations can be performed.
4) Implementation Independence allows multiple implementations for the same ADT.

Common ADTs:
1) List ADT has operations including insert, delete, search, and traverse, and can be implemented using arrays or linked lists.
2) Stack ADT has operations including push, pop, top, and isEmpty, following the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle.
3) Queue ADT has operations including enqueue, dequeue, front, and isEmpty, following the FIFO (First In, First Out) principle.
4) Set ADT has operations including add, remove, contains, union, and intersection, with no duplicate elements allowed.

Benefits of ADT:
1) Modularity separates interface from implementation.
2) Reusability provides same interface with different implementations.
3) Maintainability ensures implementation changes do not affect client code.
4) Testing allows interface to be tested independently.
examples: Stack ADT can be implemented using arrays or linked lists but provides the same push and pop operations
Queue ADT maintains FIFO ordering regardless of whether implemented with arrays or circular buffers
List ADT provides insertion and deletion operations independent of underlying storage mechanism
Set ADT guarantees uniqueness of elements regardless of implementation using hash tables or trees

sections.title: 1.1.3 Algorithm Analysis
sections.content:
Definition: Algorithm analysis is the process of determining the computational complexity of algorithms in terms of time and space requirements.

Why Analysis Matters:
1) Performance Prediction allows us to estimate how an algorithm will perform on large inputs.
2) Comparison helps choose the best algorithm among alternatives.
3) Scalability helps understand how performance changes with input size.
4) Resource Planning allows prediction of memory and time requirements.

Types of Analysis:
1) Time Complexity measures how execution time grows with input size.
2) Space Complexity measures how memory usage grows with input size.
3) Best Case represents the minimum time or space required in optimistic scenarios.
4) Average Case represents the expected time or space for typical inputs.
5) Worst Case represents the maximum time or space required in pessimistic scenarios.

Asymptotic Notation:
1) Big O notation represents the upper bound for worst case complexity.
2) Omega notation represents the lower bound for best case complexity.
3) Theta notation represents the tight bound for average case complexity.

Common Complexity Classes:
1) O(1) represents constant time for operations like array access and hash table lookup.
2) O(log n) represents logarithmic time for operations like binary search and balanced tree operations.
3) O(n) represents linear time for operations like linear search and single loops.
4) O(n log n) represents linearithmic time for efficient sorting algorithms.
5) O(n squared) represents quadratic time for nested loops and simple sorting algorithms.
6) O(2 to the power of n) represents exponential time for recursive algorithms with overlapping subproblems.

Analysis Methods:
1) Counting Operations involves counting basic operations as a function of input size.
2) Recurrence Relations are used for recursive algorithms.
3) Amortized Analysis calculates average cost over a sequence of operations.
4) Probabilistic Analysis determines expected performance on random inputs.
examples: Binary search has O(log n) time complexity and O(1) space complexity
Bubble sort has O(n squared) time complexity in worst case and O(n) in best case
Merge sort has O(n log n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity
Hash table has O(1) average access time and O(n) worst case access time

content.introduction: Complexity analysis helps estimate how algorithm performance scales with input size using time and space measures.

sections.title: Time Complexity
sections.content: Time complexity describes how runtime grows with input size. Common classes include O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), and O(n^2).
examples: Array access is O(1)
Binary search is O(log n)
Merge sort is O(n log n)

sections.title: Space Complexity
sections.content: Space complexity measures additional memory required. Some algorithms trade extra memory for speed.
examples: In-place sorts use O(1) extra space
Merge sort requires O(n) extra space

sections.title: Big O Notation
sections.content: Big O gives an upper bound on growth rate, focusing on large inputs and ignoring constants.
examples: O(n^2) dominates O(n)
O(n log n) grows slower than O(n^2)

content.introduction: An array is a container that stores objects of the same type. The objects stored in the array do not have names, but are accessed through their positions. The size of the array is fixed and cannot be changed at will.

sections.title: Define an array
sections.content:
The declaration of an array is in the form a[d], where a is the name of the array and d is the number of elements in the array. At compile time, d should be known, that is to say, d should be an integer constant expression.
And you cannot directly assign one array to another array.
examples: unsigned int d1 = 42;
const int d2 = 42;
int arr1[d1]; // Error: d1 is not a constant expression
int arr2[d2]; // Correct: arr2 is an array of length 42

sections.title: Accessing Array Elements
sections.content: You can access elements in an array using the subscript operator []. The index of an array (that is, the value inside the square brackets) starts from 0. Taking an array containing 10 elements as an example, its indexes range from 0 to 9, not from 1 to 10. However, in OI (Olympiad in Informatics), for convenience, we usually make the array a bit larger and do not use the first element of the array, starting to access array elements from index 1.
examples: int x = arr[0]; // x is now 42

sections.title: Out-of-bounds index access
sections.content: The index idx of an array should satisfy 0 < idx < size. If the index is not within this range, it is undefined behavior and can lead to unpredictable consequences, such as segmentation faults, or modifying variables unexpectedly, and so on.
examples: int arr[10];
arr[10] = 42; // Undefined behavior: out-of-bounds access
int x = arr[-1]; // Undefined behavior: out-of-bounds access

sections.title: Multidimensional Arrays
sections.content: The essence of multidimensional arrays is an 'array of arrays,' meaning the elements of the outer array are arrays. A two-dimensional array requires two dimensions to define: the length of the array and the length of the elements within the array. When accessing a two-dimensional array, two indices need to be written. We often use nested for loops to handle two-dimensional arrays.
examples: int arr[3][4]; // An array of length 3, whose elements are arrays of length 4 with elements of type int
arr[2][1] = 1; // Accessing a two-dimensional array

content.introduction: Linked lists are dynamic linear data structures where elements (nodes) are stored in arbitrary memory locations and connected through pointers. Unlike arrays, linked lists can grow or shrink during runtime and do not require contiguous memory allocation.

sections.title: Node Structure
sections.content: Each node stores data and a pointer to the next node (and optionally previous node).
examples: struct Node { int data; Node* next; }
[data|next] -> [data|next] -> NULL

sections.title: Types of Linked Lists
sections.content: Common variants include singly, doubly, and circular linked lists.
examples: Singly: A -> B -> C -> NULL
Doubly: A <-> B <-> C

sections.title: Sequential Traversal
sections.content: Random index access is not supported efficiently; traversal is from head node step by step.
examples: Access O(n)
Insert at head O(1)

content.introduction: A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last In, First Out). The most recent element added is the first one removed. Stacks are commonly used for recursion, undo/redo, and expression evaluation.

sections.title: Core Operations
sections.content: Push adds an element, pop removes the top element, and peek reads the top element without removing it.
examples: push(5)
pop()
peek()

sections.title: Implementation
sections.content: Stacks can be implemented using arrays (fixed capacity) or linked lists (dynamic).
examples: Array-based stack with top index
Linked list with head as top

sections.title: Applications
sections.content: Used in function call stack, parentheses matching, depth-first search, and undo history.
examples: Call stack frames
DFS uses stack

content.introduction: A queue is a linear data structure that follows FIFO (First In, First Out). Elements are inserted at the rear and removed from the front. Queues are used in scheduling, buffering, and BFS traversal.

sections.title: Core Operations
sections.content: Enqueue adds an element at the rear, dequeue removes the front, and peek reads the front element.
examples: enqueue(8)
dequeue()
peek()

sections.title: Variants
sections.content: Circular queues reuse space efficiently. Deques support insertion/removal at both ends.
examples: Circular queue with head/tail
Deque for sliding window

sections.title: Applications
sections.content: Queues support BFS, task scheduling, print spooling, and streaming buffers.
examples: BFS uses queue
Producer-consumer buffer

content.introduction: Pattern matching focuses on finding a pattern in text efficiently. BF and KMP are two foundational exact matching algorithms.

sections.title: BF Algorithm
sections.content: Brute Force compares pattern at each text position and restarts from next position on mismatch.
examples: Worst-case O(n*m)
Simple and intuitive implementation

sections.title: KMP Algorithm
sections.content: KMP uses prefix-suffix preprocessing (LPS/next array) to skip unnecessary comparisons.
examples: Preprocess O(m), match O(n)
Total O(n+m)

sections.title: Applications
sections.content: Used in text editors, log analysis, compiler tools, security scanning, and bioinformatics.
examples: Keyword search
Signature detection

sections.content: Given text T and pattern P, find all valid occurrences under exact or constrained matching rules.
examples: Exact match
Prefix match

sections.title: Applications
sections.content: Pattern matching is used in search engines, editors, DNA sequence analysis, log analysis, and intrusion detection.
examples: IDE find
Bioinformatics motifs

content.introduction: BF compares pattern characters one by one at each possible text offset. It is simple and intuitive, making it ideal for first-stage algorithm visualization.

sections.title: Sliding Alignment
sections.content: Align pattern at position i in text and compare sequentially.
examples: Mismatch -> i+1
Full match -> record i

sections.title: Complexity
sections.content: Worst-case complexity is O(nm) when many partial matches occur repeatedly.
examples: Text: aaaaa...
Pattern: aaaab

content.introduction: KMP improves matching by preprocessing the pattern (prefix function / next table), avoiding repeated backtracking in text. This section reserves full expansion for visualization, code demonstration, and programming practice.

sections.title: Prefix Function (LPS/Next)
sections.content: For each pattern position, store longest proper prefix that is also suffix.
examples: ababaca -> LPS sequence

sections.title: State Transition
sections.content: On mismatch, jump using prefix table instead of restarting from scratch.
examples: j fallback by LPS[j-1]

content.introduction: A tree is a hierarchical structure made of nodes and edges with a single root and no cycles. General trees allow any number of children per node and model hierarchical data such as file systems and organizational charts.

sections.title: Terminology
sections.content: Key terms include root, parent, child, sibling, leaf, subtree, degree, height, and depth.
examples: Root at top
Leaves have no children

sections.title: Tree Types
sections.content: Common variants include rooted trees, ordered trees, m-ary trees, and forests.
examples: Ordered tree preserves child order
Forest is a set of trees

sections.title: Traversal
sections.content: General trees use preorder and postorder traversals, and level-order traversal with a queue.
examples: Preorder visits parent before children
Level-order is BFS

content.introduction:
Binary trees are hierarchical structures where each node has at most two children. They model parent-child relationships and support efficient traversal and search when structured as a BST.

Key goals: represent hierarchy, enable recursive decomposition, and support tree traversals for processing nodes in different orders.

sections.title: Node and Tree Properties
sections.content: A binary tree node has left and right child references. Important properties include height, depth, subtree size, and balance. A complete tree fills levels left-to-right, while a full tree has 0 or 2 children per node.
examples: Root, internal node, leaf node
Height and depth definitions

sections.title: Traversals
sections.content: Traversal orders define how to visit nodes: preorder (root-left-right), inorder (left-root-right), postorder (left-right-root), and level-order (BFS). Each traversal is useful for different tasks.
examples: Inorder yields sorted order in BST
Level-order uses a queue

sections.title: Binary Search Tree (BST)
sections.content: In a BST, left subtree keys are smaller and right subtree keys are larger. This enables O(log n) search and insertion when balanced.
examples: Insert, search, delete in BST
Skewed BST degrades to O(n)

content.introduction: Threaded binary trees reuse null pointers to point to traversal predecessor/successor, reducing traversal overhead in specific scenarios.

sections.title: Thread Concept
sections.content: Null child pointers can be converted into predecessor/successor links.
examples: Inorder thread
Preorder thread

sections.title: Tag Bits
sections.content: Node tags distinguish child links from thread links.
examples: ltag/rtag

content.introduction: Binary Search Tree maintains ordered key property: left < root < right, enabling efficient search/update on balanced distributions.

sections.title: BST Property
sections.content: For each node, keys in left subtree are smaller and right subtree are larger.
examples: Inorder traversal is sorted

sections.title: Core Operations
sections.content: Search, insert, and delete rely on key comparisons and recursive descent.

sections.title: Rotations

sections.title: Fix-up Strategy

python.operations:
def insert_element(arr, index, value):
    new_arr = arr.copy()
    new_arr.insert(index, value)
    return new_arr

def delete_element(arr, index):
    new_arr = arr.copy()
    del new_arr[index]
    return new_arr

python.advanced:
def rotate_right(y):
    x = y.l
    t2 = x.r
    x.r = y
    y.l = t2
    y.h = 1 + max(height(y.l), height(y.r))
    x.h = 1 + max(height(x.l), height(x.r))
    return x

def split_child(x, i, t):
    y = x.child[i]
    z = BNode(y.leaf)
    z.keys = y.keys[t:]
    mid = y.keys[t - 1]
    y.keys = y.keys[:t - 1]
    x.keys.insert(i, mid)
    x.child.insert(i + 1, z)

if __name__ == '__main__':

python.basic:
def insertion_sort(arr):
    for i in range(1, len(arr)):
        key = arr[i]
        j = i - 1
        while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key:
            arr[j + 1] = arr[j]
            j -= 1
        arr[j + 1] = key


nums = [5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3]
insertion_sort(nums)
print(nums)
python.operations:
def insertion_sort_step(arr, i):
    key = arr[i]
    j = i - 1
    while j >= 0 and arr[j] > key:
        arr[j + 1] = arr[j]
        j -= 1
    arr[j + 1] = key


nums = [5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3]
insertion_sort_step(nums, 1)
insertion_sort_step(nums, 2)
print(nums)

quizTheory.introduction: This section introduces the fundamental concepts that form the foundation of data structures and algorithms. Understanding these concepts is crucial for mastering computer science and developing efficient software solutions.

sections.title: 1.1.1 Data Structures
sections.content:
Definition: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data in a computer so that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently.

Key Characteristics:
1) Organization refers to how data elements are arranged and related to each other.
2) Access Methods determine how data can be retrieved and modified.
3) Memory Usage indicates how efficiently memory is utilized.
4) Operations define the allowed operations that can be performed on the data.

Types of Data Structures:
1) Primitive Data Structures include basic data types like integers, floats, characters, and booleans.
2) Linear Data Structures have elements arranged in sequential order such as arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues.
3) Non-linear Data Structures have elements not arranged sequentially like trees, graphs, and heaps.
4) Homogeneous structures contain all elements of the same type while Heterogeneous structures contain elements of different types.

Classification by Implementation:
Static Data Structures have fixed size determined at compile time like arrays. Dynamic Data Structures can change size during runtime like linked lists and trees.
examples: Array with five elements showing linear, homogeneous, static properties
Linked List with nodes connected sequentially showing linear, dynamic properties
Binary Tree showing hierarchical structure with parent-child relationships
Hash Table showing key-value pairs with hash function mapping

sections.title: 1.1.2 Abstract Data Types (ADT)
sections.content:
Definition: An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a theoretical concept that defines a data type by its behavior (operations) rather than its implementation.

Key Properties:
1) Data Abstraction hides internal implementation details.
2) Encapsulation bundles data and operations together.
3) Interface defines what operations can be performed.
4) Implementation Independence allows multiple implementations for the same ADT.

Common ADTs:
1) List ADT has operations including insert, delete, search, and traverse, and can be implemented using arrays or linked lists.
2) Stack ADT has operations including push, pop, top, and isEmpty, following the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle.
3) Queue ADT has operations including enqueue, dequeue, front, and isEmpty, following the FIFO (First In, First Out) principle.
4) Set ADT has operations including add, remove, contains, union, and intersection, with no duplicate elements allowed.

Benefits of ADT:
1) Modularity separates interface from implementation.
2) Reusability provides same interface with different implementations.
3) Maintainability ensures implementation changes do not affect client code.
4) Testing allows interface to be tested independently.
examples: Stack ADT can be implemented using arrays or linked lists but provides the same push and pop operations
Queue ADT maintains FIFO ordering regardless of whether implemented with arrays or circular buffers
List ADT provides insertion and deletion operations independent of underlying storage mechanism
Set ADT guarantees uniqueness of elements regardless of implementation using hash tables or trees

sections.title: 1.1.3 Algorithm Analysis
sections.content:
Definition: Algorithm analysis is the process of determining the computational complexity of algorithms in terms of time and space requirements.

Why Analysis Matters:
1) Performance Prediction allows us to estimate how an algorithm will perform on large inputs.
2) Comparison helps choose the best algorithm among alternatives.
3) Scalability helps understand how performance changes with input size.
4) Resource Planning allows prediction of memory and time requirements.

Types of Analysis:
1) Time Complexity measures how execution time grows with input size.
2) Space Complexity measures how memory usage grows with input size.
3) Best Case represents the minimum time or space required in optimistic scenarios.
4) Average Case represents the expected time or space for typical inputs.
5) Worst Case represents the maximum time or space required in pessimistic scenarios.

Asymptotic Notation:
1) Big O notation represents the upper bound for worst case complexity.
2) Omega notation represents the lower bound for best case complexity.
3) Theta notation represents the tight bound for average case complexity.

Common Complexity Classes:
1) O(1) represents constant time for operations like array access and hash table lookup.
2) O(log n) represents logarithmic time for operations like binary search and balanced tree operations.
3) O(n) represents linear time for operations like linear search and single loops.
4) O(n log n) represents linearithmic time for efficient sorting algorithms.
5) O(n squared) represents quadratic time for nested loops and simple sorting algorithms.
6) O(2 to the power of n) represents exponential time for recursive algorithms with overlapping subproblems.

Analysis Methods:
1) Counting Operations involves counting basic operations as a function of input size.
2) Recurrence Relations are used for recursive algorithms.
3) Amortized Analysis calculates average cost over a sequence of operations.
4) Probabilistic Analysis determines expected performance on random inputs.
examples: Binary search has O(log n) time complexity and O(1) space complexity
Bubble sort has O(n squared) time complexity in worst case and O(n) in best case
Merge sort has O(n log n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity
Hash table has O(1) average access time and O(n) worst case access time

keyTakeaways: Data structures organize data for efficient access and manipulation
Abstract Data Types separate interface from implementation details
Algorithm analysis helps predict and compare performance
Asymptotic notation provides a mathematical framework for complexity analysis
Understanding these concepts is essential for designing efficient algorithms

questions.question: What is the primary reason array element access by index is O(1)?
options: Elements are stored in contiguous memory and addresses are computed directly
Array insertion at any position is constant time
Arrays use linked pointers between elements
questions.explanation: Array elements are in contiguous memory, so the address can be calculated directly from base address and index.

questions.question: In static arrays, the size is typically fixed at declaration time.
questions.explanation: This is true. Static arrays have a fixed size that is determined when declared.

questions.question: Which operation is usually O(n) in an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: Without sorting or indexing structures, search often scans sequentially, giving O(n).

questions.question: Why can insertion at the beginning of an array cost O(n)?
options: Because each element must be hashed
Because all existing elements may need to shift right
Because it always triggers binary search
questions.explanation: Inserting at index 0 requires moving current elements to make room, which is linear in array size.

questions.question: Deleting the last element in a typical array-backed list is generally O(1).
questions.explanation: This is generally true because no shifting is required when removing from the end.

questions.question: In a dynamic array, what does capacity represent?
options: The current number of stored elements
The allocated storage size available before resizing
questions.explanation: Capacity is the amount of allocated storage, while size is how many elements are currently stored.

questions.question: With a doubling growth strategy, dynamic array append has amortized O(1) complexity.
questions.explanation: This is true. Although occasional resizes are O(n), the average append cost over many operations is O(1).

questions.question: Briefly explain the difference between logical size and physical capacity in a dynamic array.
questions.correctAnswer: Logical size is the number of elements currently stored, while physical capacity is the total allocated slots available before resizing.
questions.explanation: Size tracks actual element count; capacity tracks allocated memory space.

quizTheory.introduction: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, providing efficient access to elements through indexing. This section covers array implementation, operations, and practical applications in computer science.

sections.title: 2.1.1 Array Fundamentals
sections.content:
Definition: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, where each element can be accessed using an index.

Key Characteristics:
1) Homogeneous Elements means all elements must be of the same data type.
2) Fixed Size indicates the size is determined at declaration time in static arrays.
3) Contiguous Memory Layout ensures elements are stored in consecutive memory addresses.
4) Index-based Access allows direct access to any element using its position.

Array Declaration and Initialization:
1) Static Arrays have fixed size determined at compile time like int arr[10].
2) Dynamic Arrays can change size during runtime using memory allocation.
3) Initialization can be done at declaration time with values like int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}.
4) Default Initialization fills the array with default values (0 for integers, null for objects).

Memory Organization:
1) Base Address is the memory address of the first element.
2) Element Size determines how much memory each element occupies.
3) Address Calculation follows the formula: address = base_address + (index �� element_size).
4) Cache Locality benefits from contiguous memory layout for better performance.

Array Types:
1) One-dimensional Arrays are linear sequences of elements.
2) Multi-dimensional Arrays are arrays of arrays, like matrices.
3) Jagged Arrays have rows of different lengths.
4) Dynamic Arrays can resize during program execution.
examples: Declaring an integer array: int numbers[5] creates space for 5 integers
Accessing elements: numbers[0] gets the first element, numbers[4] gets the last
Memory layout: If base address is 1000 and each int is 4 bytes, then numbers[2] is at address 1008
2D array: int matrix[3][4] creates a 3��4 grid of integers

sections.title: 2.1.2 Array Operations
sections.content:
Definition: Array operations are fundamental functions that can be performed on arrays to manipulate and access data.

Basic Access Operations:
1) Reading accesses an element at a specific index with O(1) time complexity.
2) Writing modifies an element at a specific index with O(1) time complexity.
3) Traversal visits all elements sequentially with O(n) time complexity.
4) Search looks for a specific element with O(n) time complexity for unsorted arrays.

Insertion Operations:
1) Insert at Beginning requires shifting all existing elements right with O(n) time complexity.
2) Insert at End simply adds element if space available with O(1) time complexity.
3) Insert at Middle requires shifting elements from insertion point to end with O(n) time complexity.
4) Resize Operation may be needed if array is full, requiring O(n) time to copy elements.

Deletion Operations:
1) Delete from Beginning requires shifting all remaining elements left with O(n) time complexity.
2) Delete from End simply removes the last element with O(1) time complexity.
3) Delete from Middle requires shifting elements from deletion point with O(n) time complexity.
4) Maintain Size may require filling deleted position with default value.

Advanced Operations:
1) Sorting arranges elements in order using algorithms like quicksort or mergesort.
2) Binary Search finds elements in sorted arrays with O(log n) time complexity.
3) Reverse changes the order of elements from last to first.
4) Rotation shifts elements by a specified number of positions.
5) Merge combines two sorted arrays into one sorted array.
examples: Reading: arr[3] retrieves the element at index 3 in constant time
Insertion at index 2: shift elements 2,3,4... to positions 3,4,5... then insert new element
Deletion at index 1: shift elements 2,3,4... to positions 1,2,3... to fill the gap
Binary search: repeatedly divide search space in half to find target element

sections.title: 2.1.3 Sequential Lists and Dynamic Arrays
sections.content:
Definition: Sequential lists are abstract data types that maintain elements in a sequence, often implemented using dynamic arrays that can resize during runtime.

Dynamic Array Implementation:
1) Capacity Management maintains actual storage size separate from logical size.
2) Growth Strategy typically doubles capacity when full to amortize resize cost.
3) Shrinking Strategy may reduce capacity when utilization drops below threshold.
4) Memory Allocation uses malloc/realloc in C or new/delete in C++ for dynamic sizing.

Capacity vs Size:
1) Logical Size represents the number of elements currently stored.
2) Physical Capacity represents the total storage space available.
3) Load Factor is the ratio of size to capacity, indicating fullness.
4) Expansion Trigger occurs when size equals capacity during insertion.

Amortized Analysis:
1) Insertion Cost is O(1) average case due to infrequent resizing operations.
2) Doubling Strategy ensures each element is copied at most log n times.
3) Total Cost for n insertions is O(n) when capacity doubles each time.
4) Average Cost per insertion becomes O(1) when amortized over all operations.

Sequential List ADT Operations:
1) Insert(index, element) adds element at specified position.
2) Delete(index) removes element at specified position.
3) Get(index) retrieves element at specified position.
4) Set(index, element) updates element at specified position.
5) Size() returns current number of elements.
6) IsEmpty() checks if list contains no elements.

Advantages and Disadvantages:
Advantages include O(1) random access, good cache locality, and simple implementation.
Disadvantages include expensive insertion/deletion in middle, fixed memory allocation, and potential memory waste.
examples: Dynamic array starts with capacity 4, grows to 8 when 5th element added
ArrayList in Java automatically manages capacity and provides sequential list interface
Vector in C++ combines dynamic arrays with additional utility functions
Python lists use dynamic arrays internally with over-allocation for efficiency

keyTakeaways: Arrays provide O(1) random access to elements using index-based addressing
Array operations have different time complexities: access O(1), insertion/deletion O(n)
Dynamic arrays automatically manage capacity and provide flexible sizing
Sequential lists combine array benefits with abstract data type interface
Memory layout and cache locality make arrays efficient for sequential access patterns

questions.question: What is a core difference between linked lists and arrays?
options: Linked lists require contiguous memory blocks
Arrays use pointers between every element
Linked list nodes can be stored in non-contiguous memory
Arrays can only store primitive data

questions.question: In a singly linked list, direct random access to the kth element is O(1).
questions.explanation: This is false. Singly linked lists require sequential traversal from the head, so access is O(k) and O(n) in general.

options: They store array indices in each node
Each node stores both next and previous pointers
questions.explanation: Given a node reference, previous and next pointers allow reconnecting neighbors in O(1).

questions.explanation: This is true. The last node links back to a node in the list, usually the head.

questions.question: Which application is commonly modeled with a circular linked list?
questions.explanation: Round-robin style processing naturally matches circular traversal behavior.

questions.question: Compared with arrays, linked lists usually have which trade-off?
options: Faster random access but slower insertions
Better cache locality and fixed-size storage
Slower random access but flexible insertions/deletions
questions.explanation: Linked lists excel at structural updates but typically have poor random access and cache locality.

questions.question: Name one common edge case that must be handled carefully in linked list operations.
questions.correctAnswer: Typical edge cases include operations on an empty list, a single-node list, or invalid positions.
questions.explanation: Robust linked list code checks boundary conditions such as empty/single-node states.

quizTheory.introduction: Linked lists are dynamic linear data structures where elements (nodes) are stored in arbitrary memory locations and connected through pointers. Unlike arrays, linked lists can grow or shrink during runtime and don't require contiguous memory allocation.

sections.title: 2.2.1 Linked List Fundamentals
sections.content:
Definition: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, and each node contains data and a reference (pointer) to the next node in the sequence.

Key Characteristics:
1) Dynamic Size allows the list to grow or shrink during program execution.
2) Non-contiguous Memory means nodes can be stored anywhere in memory.
3) Sequential Access requires traversing from the head to reach any element.
4) Pointer-based Structure uses references to connect nodes together.

Node Structure:
1) Data Field stores the actual information (can be any data type).
2) Next Pointer contains the memory address of the next node.
3) Head Pointer points to the first node in the list.
4) Tail Pointer (optional) points to the last node for efficiency.

Memory Management:
1) Dynamic Allocation uses malloc/new to allocate memory for new nodes.
2) Deallocation requires explicit freeing of memory to prevent leaks.
3) Fragmentation can occur due to non-contiguous allocation.
4) Cache Performance may be lower than arrays due to memory locality.

Types of Linked Lists:
1) Singly Linked List has nodes with one next pointer.
2) Doubly Linked List has nodes with both next and previous pointers.
3) Circular Linked List has the last node pointing back to the first.
4) Multiply Linked List has nodes with multiple pointers for different purposes.
examples: Node structure: struct Node { int data; Node* next; }
Singly linked list: [10|*] -> [20|*] -> [30|NULL]
Doubly linked list: NULL<-[*|10|*]<->[*|20|*]<->[*|30|*]->NULL
Circular linked list: [10|*] -> [20|*] -> [30|*] -> back to [10|*]

sections.title: 2.2.2 Singly Linked List Operations
sections.content:
Basic Operations on Singly Linked Lists involve manipulation of nodes and pointers with careful attention to edge cases and memory management.

Insertion Operations:
1) Insert at Beginning creates a new node, sets its next to current head, and updates head pointer. Time complexity: O(1).
2) Insert at End traverses to the last node, creates new node, and updates the last node's next pointer. Time complexity: O(n) without tail pointer, O(1) with tail pointer.
3) Insert at Position requires traversal to the desired position, creating new node, and updating pointers. Time complexity: O(n).
4) Insert After Given Node directly updates pointers without traversal. Time complexity: O(1).

Deletion Operations:
1) Delete from Beginning updates head to point to second node and frees first node. Time complexity: O(1).
2) Delete from End requires traversal to second-last node, updates its next to NULL, and frees last node. Time complexity: O(n).
3) Delete by Value searches for the node with given value and removes it. Time complexity: O(n).
4) Delete by Position traverses to the position and removes the node. Time complexity: O(n).

Traversal and Search:
1) Linear Traversal visits each node from head to tail. Time complexity: O(n).
2) Search for Value compares data in each node until found or end reached. Time complexity: O(n).
3) Count Nodes traverses the entire list counting nodes. Time complexity: O(n).
4) Display List prints all node values in sequence. Time complexity: O(n).

Edge Cases:
1) Empty List operations require null checks and proper initialization.
2) Single Node operations need special handling for head updates.
3) Memory Allocation failures should be handled gracefully.
4) Invalid Position parameters should be validated before operations.
examples: Insert at beginning: newNode->next = head; head = newNode;
Delete first node: temp = head; head = head->next; delete temp;
Search operation: while(current != NULL && current->data != value) current = current->next;
Traverse and print: while(current != NULL) { print(current->data); current = current->next; }

sections.title: 2.2.3 Doubly Linked Lists
sections.content:
Doubly Linked Lists extend singly linked lists by adding a previous pointer to each node, enabling bidirectional traversal and more efficient operations.

Node Structure:
1) Data Field stores the actual information.
2) Next Pointer points to the next node in forward direction.
3) Previous Pointer points to the previous node in backward direction.
4) Head and Tail pointers provide access to both ends of the list.

Advantages over Singly Linked Lists:
1) Bidirectional Traversal allows movement in both directions.
2) Efficient Deletion can be done in O(1) time when node reference is given.
3) Better Insert Operations can insert before a given node efficiently.
4) Reverse Traversal doesn't require recursion or additional storage.

Insertion Operations:
1) Insert at Beginning updates both head and the first node's previous pointer.
2) Insert at End updates both tail and the last node's next pointer.
3) Insert After Node updates four pointer connections for proper linking.
4) Insert Before Node can be done efficiently using the previous pointer.

Deletion Operations:
1) Delete from Beginning updates head and second node's previous pointer.
2) Delete from End updates tail and second-last node's next pointer.
3) Delete Given Node updates both neighboring nodes' pointers.
4) Memory Management requires freeing the deleted node.

Special Considerations:
1) Pointer Consistency requires updating both next and previous pointers.
2) Memory Overhead is higher due to additional pointer per node.
3) Edge Cases include operations on empty list and single node list.
4) Circular Doubly Lists have additional complexity in pointer management.
examples: Node structure: struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node* prev; }
Insert after node: newNode->next = node->next; newNode->prev = node; node->next->prev = newNode; node->next = newNode;
Delete node: node->prev->next = node->next; node->next->prev = node->prev; delete node;
Reverse traversal: while(current != NULL) { print(current->data); current = current->prev; }

sections.title: 2.2.4 Circular Linked Lists
sections.content:
Circular Linked Lists are variations where the last node points back to the first node, creating a circular structure useful for round-robin scheduling and cyclic data processing.

Types of Circular Lists:
1) Singly Circular List has the last node's next pointer pointing to the head.
2) Doubly Circular List has both forward and backward circular connections.
3) Header Node Circular List uses a dummy header node to simplify operations.
4) Multi-level Circular Lists have nodes pointing to other circular lists.

Key Properties:
1) No NULL Pointers exist in a properly formed circular list.
2) Infinite Traversal is possible without reaching a NULL pointer.
3) Any Node can serve as a starting point for traversal.
4) Memory Efficiency is similar to regular linked lists.

Operations and Considerations:
1) Traversal requires a stopping condition to avoid infinite loops.
2) Insertion maintains the circular property by updating the last node.
3) Deletion must handle the case when removing the node that external pointers reference.
4) Empty List is represented by a NULL pointer or a self-pointing node.

Applications:
1) Round-Robin Scheduling in operating systems.
2) Circular Buffers for data streaming and buffering.
3) Game Development for turn-based systems.
4) Music Playlists with repeat functionality.

Implementation Strategies:
1) Keep Track of List Size to aid in traversal and validation.
2) Use Tail Pointer instead of head for easier insertion at both ends.
3) Sentinel Nodes can simplify edge case handling.
4) Careful Testing is needed to ensure circular property is maintained.
examples: Check if circular: Use Floyd's cycle detection algorithm
Insert in circular: newNode->next = tail->next; tail->next = newNode; tail = newNode;
Traverse circular: do { process(current); current = current->next; } while(current != start);

sections.title: 2.2.5 Applications and Use Cases
sections.content:
Linked Lists are fundamental data structures with numerous practical applications in computer science and software development.

System Programming Applications:
1) Memory Management uses linked lists for free memory block tracking.
2) Process Scheduling implements ready queues and wait queues.
3) File System Organization maintains directory structures and file allocation.
4) Device Drivers use linked lists for interrupt handling and buffer management.

Data Structure Implementation:
1) Stack Implementation using linked lists provides dynamic sizing.
2) Queue Implementation avoids the size limitations of array-based queues.
3) Graph Representation uses adjacency lists with linked lists.
4) Hash Table Chaining resolves collisions using linked lists.

Algorithm Applications:
1) Polynomial Addition represents polynomials as linked lists of terms.
2) Sparse Matrix Representation stores only non-zero elements.
3) Undo Functionality in text editors and applications.
4) Browser History maintains visited page sequences.

Advanced Applications:
1) Garbage Collection algorithms use linked lists for object tracking.
2) Symbol Tables in compilers organize identifier information.
3) Expression Trees can be implemented using linked structures.
4) Database Indexing uses linked structures for B-trees and other indices.

Performance Considerations:
1) Memory Access Patterns affect cache performance compared to arrays.
2) Dynamic Allocation provides flexibility but adds overhead.
3) Pointer Traversal is sequential, limiting random access performance.
4) Memory Fragmentation can occur with frequent allocation/deallocation.

When to Use Linked Lists:
1) Unknown or Variable Size data requirements.
2) Frequent Insertion/Deletion operations, especially at the beginning.
3) Implementation of Other Data Structures like stacks and queues.
4) Memory is Fragmented or contiguous allocation is not possible.
examples: Undo system: Each action stored as a node in a doubly linked list
Music playlist: Circular linked list for continuous play with repeat
Process scheduling: Ready queue implemented as a linked list

keyTakeaways: Linked lists provide dynamic memory allocation and flexible sizing
Different types serve different purposes: singly, doubly, and circular
Operations have different time complexities compared to arrays
Memory management and pointer manipulation are crucial skills
Choose the right type based on specific application requirements

quizQuestions.question: What is a core difference between linked lists and arrays?
options: Linked lists require contiguous memory blocks
Arrays use pointers between every element
Linked list nodes can be stored in non-contiguous memory
Arrays can only store primitive data
quizQuestions.explanation: Linked list nodes are connected by pointers and do not require contiguous memory.

quizQuestions.question: In a singly linked list, direct random access to the kth element is O(1).
quizQuestions.explanation: This is false. Singly linked lists require sequential traversal from the head, so access is O(k) and O(n) in general.

quizQuestions.question: Which singly linked list operation is O(1) when implemented with only a head pointer?
quizQuestions.explanation: Insert-at-beginning only updates the new node's next and head pointer, so it is constant time.

quizQuestions.question: Why can doubly linked lists support efficient deletion of a known node?
options: They store array indices in each node
Each node stores both next and previous pointers
quizQuestions.explanation: Given a node reference, previous and next pointers allow reconnecting neighbors in O(1).

quizQuestions.question: A proper circular linked list has no NULL next pointer in the cycle.
quizQuestions.explanation: This is true. The last node links back to a node in the list, usually the head.

quizQuestions.question: Which application is commonly modeled with a circular linked list?
quizQuestions.explanation: Round-robin style processing naturally matches circular traversal behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Compared with arrays, linked lists usually have which trade-off?
options: Faster random access but slower insertions
Better cache locality and fixed-size storage
Slower random access but flexible insertions/deletions
quizQuestions.explanation: Linked lists excel at structural updates but typically have poor random access and cache locality.

quizQuestions.question: Name one common edge case that must be handled carefully in linked list operations.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Typical edge cases include operations on an empty list, a single-node list, or invalid positions.
quizQuestions.explanation: Robust linked list code checks boundary conditions such as empty/single-node states.

quizTheory.introduction: Strings are one of the most common linear data structures in software systems. This section covers what strings are, how they are stored in memory, and how basic operations affect correctness and performance.

sections.title: 3.1.1 String Definition and Characteristics
sections.content:
Definition: A string is a finite sequence of characters arranged in linear order. Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or Unicode code points.

Core Characteristics:
1) Ordered sequence: character positions matter.
2) Length-aware: each string has a defined length.
3) Character set dependent: ASCII, UTF-8, UTF-16, etc.
4) Immutable or mutable by language design.

String as a Linear Structure:
- Adjacent logical positions (index 0, 1, 2...) make strings a typical linear structure.
- Many algorithms depend on sequential access and indexing.
examples: "hello" has length 5 with indices 0..4
"data-structures" can be represented with Unicode code points
An empty string "" has length 0

sections.title: 3.1.2 Storage Models and Memory Representation
sections.content:
Common Storage Models:
1) Fixed-length arrays reserve a constant buffer size.
2) Dynamic arrays allow resizing as content grows.
3) Linked representation stores characters in linked nodes (less common in modern systems).

Encoding and Memory:
- ASCII uses 1 byte for standard English characters.
- UTF-8 uses variable length and is space-efficient for ASCII-heavy text.
- UTF-16 often used internally in many runtimes.

Practical Trade-offs:
- Contiguous storage improves cache locality and random index access.
- Linked storage simplifies some insert/delete operations but increases pointer overhead.
- Encoding choice affects length calculation and substring indexing semantics.
examples: C-style string uses char array ending with null terminator
Java String stores characters in internal arrays with encoding metadata
UTF-8 string length in bytes may differ from character count

sections.title: 3.1.3 Basic String Operations
sections.content:
Frequent Operations:
1) Access: read character by index.
2) Traversal: iterate over each character.
3) Comparison: lexicographical or equality check.
4) Concatenation: combine multiple strings.
5) Substring: extract a contiguous segment.
6) Insertion/Deletion/Replacement: modify content (or create new string for immutable types).

Complexity Overview (typical contiguous model):
- Index access: O(1)
- Traversal: O(n)
- Comparison: O(min(n, m))
- Concatenation: O(n + m) unless optimized by builders
- Substring copy: O(k)

Best Practices:
- Use string builders/buffers for repeated concatenation.
- Understand immutability to avoid hidden allocation costs.
- Be explicit about character encoding in I/O boundaries.
examples: Comparing two equal long strings may scan all characters
Repeated + in loops can cause quadratic allocation overhead
Substring extraction in UTF-8 should respect character boundaries

keyTakeaways: String is a classic linear structure organized by character positions
Storage model and encoding directly affect performance and correctness
Basic operations have predictable complexity patterns
Immutability is safe but may introduce extra memory allocation
Efficient string handling requires choosing proper APIs for the workload

quizTheory.introduction: Pattern matching solves the problem of finding a pattern string inside a text string. It is fundamental in text processing, search systems, compilers, and bioinformatics.

sections.title: 3.2.1 Problem Definition and Terminology
sections.content:
Given text T (length n) and pattern P (length m), determine whether P appears in T, and if so, return occurrence positions.

Terminology:
1) Exact matching: characters must match exactly.
2) First match vs all matches: return first index or all valid indices.
3) Prefix and suffix: key concepts used in advanced algorithms.

Challenges:
- Large text sizes make naive approaches expensive.
- Repeated prefixes in pattern can be exploited to skip redundant comparisons.
examples: Text: ABABCABCACBAB, Pattern: ABCAC
Find all occurrences of "ana" in "banana"
Search keyword in log files with millions of lines

sections.title: 3.2.2 BF (Brute Force) Algorithm
sections.content:
Core Idea:
Align pattern at each possible text position and compare characters one by one. On mismatch, shift pattern by one position and restart comparison.

Process:
1) Set i at current text start, j at pattern start.
2) If T[i + j] == P[j], continue j++.
3) If mismatch, move start to i + 1 and reset j.
4) If j reaches m, a match is found.

Complexity:
- Worst case: O(n * m)
- Best case: O(n) for early mismatches
- Space: O(1)

Advantages and Limitations:
- Very simple and easy to implement.
- Can be slow on repetitive text/pattern combinations.
examples: Pattern "aaaab" in text "aaaaaaaaab" causes many repeated comparisons
Small inputs often make BF acceptable
BF is useful as a baseline for correctness testing

sections.title: 3.2.3 KMP Algorithm
sections.content:
Core Idea:
When mismatch occurs, avoid rechecking known-equal characters by using prefix information of the pattern.

Key Preprocessing:
- Build next/LPS array (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix).
- LPS tells where to continue in pattern after mismatch.

Matching Phase:
1) Compare text and pattern pointers.
2) On match, advance both pointers.
3) On mismatch, move pattern pointer using LPS instead of resetting to zero.
4) Continue until text ends or full match found.

Complexity:
- Preprocessing: O(m)
- Matching: O(n)
- Total: O(n + m), Space: O(m)

Why KMP is Better:
- Eliminates redundant comparisons in repetitive patterns.
- Provides stable linear-time performance.
examples: Pattern "ABABC" has reusable prefix-suffix structure
KMP performs well in long document search
KMP is a core technique for efficient exact matching

sections.title: 3.2.4 Applications of Pattern Matching
sections.content:
Typical Application Domains:
1) Text editor search and replace.
2) Log and monitoring system keyword detection.
3) DNA/protein sequence analysis.
4) Intrusion detection and signature scanning.
5) Compiler lexical processing and token recognition.

Engineering Considerations:
- Choose BF for simple, small-scale scenarios.
- Choose KMP when predictable linear performance is required.
- For multi-pattern or fuzzy matching, use specialized algorithms (e.g., Aho-Corasick, regex engines).
examples: IDE search panel finds symbol occurrences in source files
SIEM platform detects known malicious signatures
Bioinformatics pipeline scans genome subsequences

keyTakeaways: Pattern matching locates pattern occurrences in text efficiently
BF is simple but can degrade to O(n * m)
KMP uses prefix-suffix preprocessing to achieve O(n + m)
Algorithm choice should match data scale and performance requirements
Pattern matching is widely used across software and scientific systems

quizTheory.introduction: Heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies a global order constraint between parent and child nodes. It is a core structure for priority queues, task scheduling, top-k queries, and graph algorithms like Dijkstra and Prim.

sections.title: 4.10.1 Definition and Core Properties
sections.content:
A heap is a complete binary tree:
1) All levels except possibly the last are full.
2) Last level is filled from left to right.

Heap-order property:
- Max-heap: parent >= children.
- Min-heap: parent <= children.

Because of completeness, heap is naturally stored in an array and does not require explicit pointers.
examples: Max-heap root is always the current maximum
Min-heap root is always the current minimum
Complete-tree shape makes index-based navigation possible

sections.title: 4.10.2 Array Representation and Index Rules
sections.content:
For 0-based arrays:
- Parent index of i: (i - 1) / 2 
- Left child of i: 2i + 1
- Right child of i: 2i + 2

Advantages:
- O(1) access to parent and children by index formula.
- Better cache locality than pointer-based trees.

Boundary checks are essential when child index exceeds array length.
examples: Node at index 3 has parent index 1
Node at index 2 has children indices 5 and 6
Array [50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 35] is a valid max-heap

sections.title: 4.10.3 Fundamental Heap Operations
sections.content:
1) Insert (push): append element to tail, then sift-up until heap property is restored.
2) Extract-top (pop): remove root, move last element to root, then sift-down.
3) Peek: read root directly.
4) Heapify build: build heap from an unsorted array bottom-up.

Complexities:
- Insert: O(log n)
- Extract-top: O(log n)
- Peek: O(1)
- Build heap (bottom-up): O(n)
examples: Insert 60 into max-heap and move it upward by swaps
Pop max value then sink replacement element down
Build heap faster with bottom-up heapify than repeated insert

sections.title: 4.10.4 Typical Applications and Comparison
sections.content:
Typical scenarios:
- Priority queues (task scheduling, event simulation).
- Top-k and streaming extremes.
- Heap sort.
- Support structures in Dijkstra/Prim.

Comparison notes:
- Compared with balanced BST, heap gives faster top access but does not support ordered traversal efficiently.
- Compared with unsorted arrays, heap significantly improves repeated top extraction.
examples: Real-time scheduler picks highest-priority job from heap
Top-100 hot items maintained with a fixed-size min-heap
Heap sort uses max-heap to produce ascending order

keyTakeaways: Heap combines complete-tree shape with parent-child order constraints
Array representation makes heap operations simple and efficient
Sift-up and sift-down are the core maintenance procedures
Build-heap can be done in linear time O(n)
Heap is the standard implementation base for priority queues
